text.title: Fallback-chain semantics and what goes in the chain
text.summary: How the gateway turns a single call into an ordered try-list across providers, what counts as a fallback trigger, and what NOT to put in the chain.

section.title: Fallback semantics
section.p: The gateway accepts a primary model plus an ordered list of alternates. On a fallback-eligible error from the primary (5xx, 429, timeout, content-policy refusal, or empty completion), it transparently retries the next model in the list with the same messages. Only the successful call is billed.

rule.statement: Configure every production LLM call with a primary model AND at least one fallback model from a different provider; same-vendor fallbacks do not protect against vendor-wide incidents.
rule.rationale: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google have all had multi-hour outages affecting all of their models simultaneously; cross-provider fallbacks are the only path to availability above the single-vendor floor.

example.code: client.chat.completions.create( model="anthropic/claude-opus-4", messages=messages, extra_body={"models": [ "anthropic/claude-opus-4", # primary "openai/gpt-5", # cross-provider fallback "google/gemini-2.5-pro", # second fallback ]}, )
example.note: Three providers, ordered by quality; Anthropic outage routes to OpenAI; OpenAI rate limit routes to Gemini.
All three depend on Anthropic infrastructure; a vendor-wide outage takes them all down at once.

section.reference: OpenRouter — model routing and Auto Router.
OpenRouter — model fallbacks and Zero Completion Insurance.

section.title: What counts as a fallback trigger

list.item: HTTP 5xx from the upstream provider — retry next.
HTTP 429 rate limit — retry next, optionally after a short backoff if no other provider is configured.
Network timeout (default 60s) — retry next with the same message body.
Empty completion or content-policy refusal — retry next ONLY if the policy on this path says so; otherwise surface the refusal.

rule.statement: Surface 4xx client errors (400 bad request, 401 unauthenticated, 422 validation) without fallback; these are agent bugs, not provider incidents, and silently retrying masks them.
rule.rationale: Falling back on a 400 means you ship a broken request to every provider in the chain, multiplying the failure surface and hiding the bug from observability.

antipattern.description: Fallback chain with the same model from three different routers (e.g., direct, OpenRouter, AWS Bedrock).
antipattern.reason: Same model means same content-policy refusal and same outage class; you pay three integrations for one logical fallback.

section.reference: Reference call wiring with primary + cross-provider fallbacks.

see-also.link: Compliance, vendor-feature parity, and observability trade-offs.
